Psychological Measurement: Descriptive Statistics, Visualizations, and Reliability Tutorial

Christopher J. Schmank, PhD

2026-03-03

Load Libraries for Tutorial

library(tidyverse)
library(psych)
library(jmv)

Data Visualization Template ggplot

ggplot(data = <DATA>, 
       mapping = aes(<MAPPINGS>)) + 
  <GEOM_FUNCTION>()
  • data = name of data frame/object within R Environment
  • mapping = aes() argument requires variable name/label from within data =
    • What variable(s) do you want to visualize?
    • Different graphics/visualizations require specifying:
      • x = OR y = within aes() (univariate)
      • x = AND y = within aes() (bivariate/grouped)
  • geom_function() is then layered (+) into the code to achieve the appropriate visualization:
    • Histograms — geom_histogram()
    • Boxplots — geom_boxplot()
    • Scatterplots — geom_point()
    • Linegraphs — geom_line()
    • Barplots — geom_bar() OR geom_col()

Data Visualization | Histograms & Boxplots

Histogram Examples:

# Univariate Histogram Code
ggplot(data = bfi,
       mapping = aes(x = age)) +
  geom_histogram() 

# Faceted, Bivariate Histogram Code
ggplot(data = bfi,
       mapping = aes(x = age)) +
  geom_histogram() +
  facet_wrap(~ gender, nrow = 2) # Can also use `ncol = 1` for same stacked visualization

Histogram Examples Expanded

# Adding Cutomization Layers to Histogram Visualization
ggplot(data = bfi,
       mapping = aes(x = age)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 12, color = "goldenrod2") +
  labs(y = "Count", x = "Age of Participant")

bfi$genderF<-factor(bfi$gender, labels = c("Male", "Female"))

ggplot(data = bfi,
       mapping = aes(x = age)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 12, color = "forestgreen") +
  facet_wrap(~ genderF, nrow = 2) +
  labs(y = "Count", x = "Age of Participant")

Boxplot Examples

# Univariate boxplot
ggplot(data = bfi,
       mapping = aes(y = age)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(y = "Age of Participant")

#Grouped, Bivariate Boxplot
ggplot(data = bfi,
       mapping = aes(x = genderF, y = age)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(x = "Gender", y = "Age of Participant")

Data Visualization | Scatterplots & Linegraphs

Scatterplot Examples

# Bivariate Scatterplot
ggplot(data = attitude, 
       mapping = aes(x = complaints, y = rating)) + 
  geom_point() +
  labs(x = "How Employee Deals With Complaints Rating", y = "Overall Employee Ratings")

# Bivariate Scatterplot w/ Line of Best-Fit
ggplot(data = attitude, 
       mapping = aes(x = complaints, y = rating)) + 
  geom_point() +
  labs(x = "How Employee Deals With Complaints Rating", y = "Overall Employee Ratings") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)

# Bivariate Scatterplot w/ Line of Best-Fit (+ Standard Error)
ggplot(data = attitude, 
       mapping = aes(x = complaints, y = rating)) + 
  geom_point() +
  labs(x = "How Employee Deals With Complaints Rating", y = "Overall Employee Ratings") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE)

Linegraph Example

# Bivariate Linegraph (TIME ACROSS X-AXIS)
ggplot(data = economics, 
       aes(x = date, y = unemploy)) +
  geom_line() + 
  labs(x = "Data Collection Year", y = "Number of Unemployed Persons")

Describing Data | psych Package

# Describe entire dataset OR subset of data object
describe(<DATA>)

# Grouped descriptive statistics of entire dataset OR subset of data object
describeBy(<DATA>, 
           group = <GROUPING VARIABLE>,
           mat = TRUE)

By default describe() and describeBy() functions use these arguments:

  1. Remove missing data listwise — na.rm = TRUE
  2. Calculate skew and kurtosis — skew = TRUE
  3. Calculate the range — ranges = TRUE
  4. DOES NOT calculate the Interquartile Range — IQR = FALSE
describe(bfi[,c(1:5,26,28)], 
         IQR = TRUE)
##        vars    n  mean    sd median trimmed   mad min max range  skew kurtosis
## A1        1 2784  2.41  1.41      2    2.23  1.48   1   6     5  0.83    -0.31
## A2        2 2773  4.80  1.17      5    4.98  1.48   1   6     5 -1.12     1.05
## A3        3 2774  4.60  1.30      5    4.79  1.48   1   6     5 -1.00     0.44
## A4        4 2781  4.70  1.48      5    4.93  1.48   1   6     5 -1.03     0.04
## A5        5 2784  4.56  1.26      5    4.71  1.48   1   6     5 -0.85     0.16
## gender    6 2800  1.67  0.47      2    1.71  0.00   1   2     1 -0.73    -1.47
## age       7 2800 28.78 11.13     26   27.43 10.38   3  86    83  1.02     0.56
##          se IQR
## A1     0.03   2
## A2     0.02   2
## A3     0.02   2
## A4     0.03   2
## A5     0.02   1
## gender 0.01   1
## age    0.21  15
describeBy(bfi[,c(1:5,28:29)],
           group = bfi$genderF,
           IQR = TRUE,
           mat = TRUE)
##          item group1 vars    n      mean        sd median   trimmed     mad min
## A11         1   Male    1  918  2.728758  1.426292      2  2.615489  1.4826   1
## A12         2 Female    1 1866  2.258307  1.372557      2  2.050870  1.4826   1
## A21         3   Male    2  908  4.496696  1.263075      5  4.653846  1.4826   1
## A22         4 Female    2 1865  4.951206  1.094896      5  5.121902  1.4826   1
## A31         5   Male    3  912  4.343202  1.327567      5  4.473973  1.4826   1
## A32         6 Female    3 1862  4.731472  1.270027      5  4.928188  1.4826   1
## A41         7   Male    4  916  4.433406  1.481005      5  4.604905  1.4826   1
## A42         8 Female    4 1865  4.830563  1.461681      5  5.092431  1.4826   1
## A51         9   Male    5  915  4.379235  1.319524      5  4.510232  1.4826   1
## A52        10 Female    5 1869  4.649010  1.218116      5  4.800267  1.4826   1
## age1       11   Male    6  919 28.022851 11.033348     25 26.488467  8.8956   3
## age2       12 Female    6 1881 29.153110 11.157430     26 27.900997 10.3782   9
## genderF1   13   Male    7  919  1.000000  0.000000      1  1.000000  0.0000   1
## genderF2   14 Female    7 1881  2.000000  0.000000      2  2.000000  0.0000   2
##          max range       skew    kurtosis         se IQR
## A11        6     5  0.5576274 -0.68994248 0.04707464   2
## A12        6     5  0.9888299  0.03766298 0.03177419   2
## A21        6     5 -0.8910928  0.33927504 0.04191662   1
## A22        6     5 -1.2417407  1.55103886 0.02535325   2
## A31        6     5 -0.7493391 -0.11499484 0.04396014   1
## A32        6     5 -1.1519156  0.89657199 0.02943223   2
## A41        6     5 -0.7625627 -0.39346565 0.04893377   2
## A42        6     5 -1.1958025  0.40652818 0.03384646   2
## A51        6     5 -0.7199403 -0.20153783 0.04362212   1
## A52        6     5 -0.9048272  0.36371426 0.02817630   2
## age1      74    71  1.2344546  1.21849551 0.36395657  14
## age2      86    77  0.9261884  0.29488949 0.25725853  16
## genderF1   1     0        NaN         NaN 0.00000000   0
## genderF2   2     0        NaN         NaN 0.00000000   0

Calculating Correlation | psych and jmv

# `psych` package correlation matrix
cor_data <- corr.test(<DATA>,
                      use = "complete")
print(cor_data, short = FALSE)

# Can call for ONLY correlation matrix OR save as data object
cor_data$r

# `jmv` package correlation matrix and visualization
corrMatrix(<DATA>, 
           vars = vars(<VARIABLE1>, <VARIABLE2>, <VARIABLE3>, ... <VARIABLEk>,),
           ci = TRUE,
           plots = TRUE,
           plotDens=TRUE,
           plotStats=TRUE)

psych Correlation Example

# Generate correlation data object 
cor_bfi <- corr.test(bfi[,c(1:5,26,28)],
                     use = "complete")

# Prints all output related to `corr.test()`
print(cor_bfi, short=FALSE)
## Call:corr.test(x = bfi[, c(1:5, 26, 28)], use = "complete")
## Correlation matrix 
##           A1    A2    A3    A4    A5 gender   age
## A1      1.00 -0.34 -0.27 -0.15 -0.18  -0.16 -0.16
## A2     -0.34  1.00  0.49  0.34  0.39   0.18  0.11
## A3     -0.27  0.49  1.00  0.36  0.51   0.14  0.07
## A4     -0.15  0.34  0.36  1.00  0.31   0.13  0.14
## A5     -0.18  0.39  0.51  0.31  1.00   0.10  0.12
## gender -0.16  0.18  0.14  0.13  0.10   1.00  0.05
## age    -0.16  0.11  0.07  0.14  0.12   0.05  1.00
## Sample Size 
## [1] 2751
## Probability values (Entries above the diagonal are adjusted for multiple tests.) 
##        A1 A2 A3 A4 A5 gender  age
## A1      0  0  0  0  0   0.00 0.00
## A2      0  0  0  0  0   0.00 0.00
## A3      0  0  0  0  0   0.00 0.00
## A4      0  0  0  0  0   0.00 0.00
## A5      0  0  0  0  0   0.00 0.00
## gender  0  0  0  0  0   0.00 0.01
## age     0  0  0  0  0   0.01 0.00
## 
##  Confidence intervals based upon normal theory.  To get bootstrapped values, try cor.ci
##           raw.lower raw.r raw.upper raw.p lower.adj upper.adj
## A1-A2         -0.37 -0.34     -0.31  0.00     -0.39     -0.29
## A1-A3         -0.30 -0.27     -0.23  0.00     -0.32     -0.22
## A1-A4         -0.18 -0.15     -0.11  0.00     -0.20     -0.10
## A1-A5         -0.22 -0.18     -0.15  0.00     -0.24     -0.13
## A1-gendr      -0.20 -0.16     -0.12  0.00     -0.21     -0.11
## A1-age        -0.20 -0.16     -0.12  0.00     -0.21     -0.11
## A2-A3          0.46  0.49      0.51  0.00      0.44      0.53
## A2-A4          0.30  0.34      0.37  0.00      0.28      0.38
## A2-A5          0.36  0.39      0.42  0.00      0.34      0.44
## A2-gendr       0.15  0.18      0.22  0.00      0.13      0.23
## A2-age         0.08  0.11      0.15  0.00      0.07      0.16
## A3-A4          0.33  0.36      0.39  0.00      0.31      0.41
## A3-A5          0.48  0.51      0.53  0.00      0.46      0.55
## A3-gendr       0.10  0.14      0.17  0.00      0.09      0.19
## A3-age         0.03  0.07      0.10  0.00      0.02      0.11
## A4-A5          0.27  0.31      0.34  0.00      0.26      0.36
## A4-gendr       0.09  0.13      0.16  0.00      0.08      0.17
## A4-age         0.11  0.14      0.18  0.00      0.09      0.19
## A5-gendr       0.06  0.10      0.14  0.00      0.06      0.15
## A5-age         0.09  0.12      0.16  0.00      0.08      0.17
## gendr-age      0.01  0.05      0.09  0.01      0.01      0.09
# Prints only correlation matrix
cor_bfi$r
##                A1         A2          A3         A4         A5      gender
## A1      1.0000000 -0.3416242 -0.26828171 -0.1483927 -0.1826790 -0.16121587
## A2     -0.3416242  1.0000000  0.48675035  0.3352432  0.3877875  0.18238742
## A3     -0.2682817  0.4867503  1.00000000  0.3621720  0.5051762  0.13643325
## A4     -0.1483927  0.3352432  0.36217201  1.0000000  0.3067003  0.12576692
## A5     -0.1826790  0.3877875  0.50517620  0.3067003  1.0000000  0.10189236
## gender -0.1612159  0.1823874  0.13643325  0.1257669  0.1018924  1.00000000
## age    -0.1605245  0.1135578  0.06626393  0.1430789  0.1249933  0.04840299
##                age
## A1     -0.16052453
## A2      0.11355781
## A3      0.06626393
## A4      0.14307890
## A5      0.12499326
## gender  0.04840299
## age     1.00000000
# Saves correlation matrix as data object for later use
cor_bfi_r <- cor_bfi$r

jmv Correlation Example

# Can specify the variables individually
corrMatrix(bfi,
           vars = vars('A1','A2','A3','A4','A5','gender','age'),
           ci=TRUE,
           plots = TRUE,
           plotDens=TRUE,
           plotStats=TRUE)
## 
##  CORRELATION MATRIX
## 
##  Correlation Matrix                                                                                                         
##  ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 
##                              A1            A2            A3            A4            A5            gender       age         
##  ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── 
##    A1        Pearson's r              —                                                                                     
##              df                       —                                                                                     
##              p-value                  —                                                                                     
##              95% CI Upper             —                                                                                     
##              95% CI Lower             —                                                                                     
##                                                                                                                             
##    A2        Pearson's r     -0.3401932             —                                                                       
##              df                    2755             —                                                                       
##              p-value         < .0000001             —                                                                       
##              95% CI Upper    -0.3067584             —                                                                       
##              95% CI Lower    -0.3727895             —                                                                       
##                                                                                                                             
##    A3        Pearson's r     -0.2652471     0.4850980             —                                                         
##              df                    2757          2749             —                                                         
##              p-value         < .0000001    < .0000001             —                                                         
##              95% CI Upper    -0.2302087     0.5131662             —                                                         
##              95% CI Lower    -0.2995986     0.4559934             —                                                         
##                                                                                                                             
##    A4        Pearson's r     -0.1464245     0.3350872     0.3604283             —                                           
##              df                    2765          2756          2757             —                                           
##              p-value         < .0000001    < .0000001    < .0000001             —                                           
##              95% CI Upper    -0.1097604     0.3678109     0.3924666             —                                           
##              95% CI Lower    -0.1826907     0.3015346     0.3275164             —                                           
##                                                                                                                             
##    A5        Pearson's r     -0.1814383     0.3900836     0.5041411     0.3075373             —                             
##              df                    2767          2755          2756          2763             —                             
##              p-value         < .0000001    < .0000001    < .0000001    < .0000001             —                             
##              95% CI Upper    -0.1451698     0.4212794     0.5314646     0.3409056             —                             
##              95% CI Lower    -0.2172197     0.3579658     0.4757697     0.2733950             —

##              df                    2782          2771          2772          2779          2782            —                
##              p-value         < .0000001    < .0000001    < .0000001    < .0000001     0.0000001            —

##              df                    2782          2771          2772          2779          2782         2798            —   
##              p-value         < .0000001    < .0000001     0.0002709    < .0000001    < .0000001    0.0115850            —   
##              95% CI Upper    -0.1245595     0.1508577     0.1060337     0.1804489     0.1654491    0.0845967            —

##  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# Can specify the variables by subsetting data object
corrMatrix(bfi[,c(1:5,26,28)],
           ci=TRUE,
           plots = TRUE,
           plotDens=TRUE,
           plotStats=TRUE)

Internal Consistency Reliability

Cronbach’s Alpha (\(\alpha\))

# Set seed for replicability of bootstrapped CI generation
set.seed(<NUMBER>)

# Create alpha data object for later use
alpha_dat<-alpha(<DATA>,
                 check.keys = TRUE,
                 n.iter = 100,
                 use = "complete.obs")

Alpha Example

# For replication, value of `set.seed()` should remain consistent 
set.seed(13)

# Creates data object that can be printed fully or called specifically
alpha_bfi <- alpha(bfi[1:25],
                   check.keys = TRUE,
                   n.iter = 100,
                   use="complete.obs")

# Call all output related to alpha function
alpha_bfi
## 
## Reliability analysis   
## Call: alpha(x = bfi[1:25], check.keys = TRUE, n.iter = 100, use = "complete.obs")
## 
##   raw_alpha std.alpha G6(smc) average_r S/N    ase mean   sd median_r
##       0.82      0.82    0.87      0.16 4.6 0.0047  4.2 0.61     0.13
## 
##     95% confidence boundaries 
##              lower alpha upper
## Feldt         0.82  0.82  0.83
## Duhachek      0.82  0.82  0.83
## bootstrapped  0.81  0.82  0.83
## 
##  Reliability if an item is dropped:
##     raw_alpha std.alpha G6(smc) average_r S/N alpha se var.r med.r
## A1-      0.83      0.82    0.87      0.16 4.7   0.0047 0.021  0.13
## A2       0.82      0.82    0.86      0.16 4.4   0.0049 0.020  0.13
## A3       0.82      0.81    0.86      0.15 4.4   0.0050 0.020  0.13
## A4       0.82      0.82    0.86      0.16 4.5   0.0049 0.021  0.13
## A5       0.81      0.81    0.86      0.15 4.3   0.0050 0.020  0.12
## C1       0.82      0.82    0.86      0.16 4.5   0.0048 0.021  0.13
## C2       0.82      0.82    0.86      0.16 4.5   0.0048 0.020  0.13
## C3       0.82      0.82    0.86      0.16 4.6   0.0048 0.021  0.13
## C4-      0.81      0.81    0.86      0.15 4.3   0.0050 0.021  0.13
## C5-      0.81      0.81    0.86      0.15 4.3   0.0050 0.021  0.12
## E1-      0.82      0.82    0.86      0.16 4.5   0.0049 0.020  0.13
## E2-      0.81      0.81    0.85      0.15 4.2   0.0052 0.020  0.12
## E3       0.82      0.81    0.86      0.15 4.4   0.0050 0.020  0.13
## E4       0.81      0.81    0.86      0.15 4.3   0.0051 0.019  0.13
## E5       0.82      0.81    0.86      0.15 4.3   0.0050 0.020  0.12
## N1-      0.82      0.82    0.86      0.16 4.4   0.0050 0.019  0.13
## N2-      0.82      0.82    0.86      0.16 4.4   0.0049 0.019  0.13
## N3-      0.82      0.82    0.86      0.16 4.5   0.0049 0.019  0.13
## N4-      0.81      0.81    0.86      0.15 4.3   0.0051 0.020  0.13
## N5-      0.82      0.82    0.86      0.16 4.5   0.0049 0.020  0.13

## O1  2778 0.3529598 0.3780276 0.33295459 0.28707912 4.816055 1.129530
## O2- 2800 0.2967217 0.2917613 0.23613282 0.20175624 4.286786 1.565152
## O3  2772 0.4168522 0.4394659 0.40851790 0.34965100 4.438312 1.220901
## O4- 2786 0.1238701 0.1160061 0.04367637 0.04734341 2.107681 1.221250
## O5- 2780 0.2760027 0.2852538 0.23084073 0.19464771 4.510432 1.327959
# Can use dplyr piping to retrieve Standardized Alpha value
bfi %>%
  dplyr::select(A1:O5) %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  alpha(check.keys = TRUE) %>% # check.keys = TRUE checks for negatively coded items and reverses them!!
  .$total %>% 
  dplyr::select("std.alpha")
##  std.alpha
##  0.8229456

McDonald’s Omega (\(\omega\))

# Create omega data object for later use
omega_dat <- omega(<DATA>,
                   check.keys=TRUE,
                   nfactors = <NUMBER>,
                   fm = "ml",
                   n.iter = 1,
                   use = "complete.obs")

Omega Example

# Creates data object that can be printed fully or called separately
omega_bfi <- omega(bfi[1:25],
                   check.keys=TRUE,
                   nfactors=5,
                   fm="ml",
                   n.iter=1,
                   use = "complete.obs")

# Calls all output related to omega function
omega_bfi
## Omega 
## Call: omegah(m = m, nfactors = nfactors, fm = fm, key = key, flip = flip, 
##     digits = digits, title = title, sl = sl, labels = labels, 
##     plot = plot, n.obs = n.obs, rotate = rotate, Phi = Phi, option = option, 
##     covar = covar, check.keys = TRUE, use = "complete.obs")
## Alpha:                 0.82 
## G.6:                   0.86 
## Omega Hierarchical:    0.47 
## Omega H asymptotic:    0.53 
## Omega Total            0.87 
## 
## Schmid Leiman Factor loadings greater than  0.2 
##         g   F1*   F2*   F3*   F4*   F5*    h2   h2   u2   p2  com
## A1-       -0.20              0.31             0.15 0.85 0.14 2.56
## A2   0.35                    0.53        0.40 0.40 0.60 0.30 1.80
## A3   0.41                    0.58        0.51 0.51 0.49 0.33 1.84
## A4   0.30                    0.40        0.29 0.29 0.71 0.31 2.66
## A5   0.45                    0.50        0.48 0.48 0.52 0.41 2.32
## C1   0.24              0.48              0.32 0.32 0.68 0.18 1.74
## C2   0.24              0.57              0.43 0.43 0.57 0.14 1.61
## C3   0.22              0.51              0.32 0.32 0.68 0.15 1.52
## C4-  0.31              0.58              0.47 0.47 0.53 0.21 1.66
## C5-  0.34              0.51              0.43 0.43 0.57 0.27 2.15
## E1-  0.36        0.45                    0.37 0.37 0.63 0.34 2.28
## E2-  0.49        0.54                    0.55 0.55 0.45 0.44 2.09
## E3   0.44        0.27        0.26  0.29  0.44 0.44 0.56 0.44 3.30
## E4   0.48        0.42        0.32        0.52 0.52 0.48 0.44 2.75
## E5   0.42        0.32  0.25        0.21  0.40 0.40 0.60 0.44 3.62
## N1-  0.24 -0.82                          0.71 0.71 0.29 0.08 1.22
## N2-  0.24 -0.78                          0.66 0.66 0.34 0.09 1.21
## N3-  0.24 -0.64                          0.53 0.53 0.47 0.11 1.40
## N4-  0.36 -0.40  0.34                    0.48 0.48 0.52 0.27 3.41
## N5-  0.22 -0.42  0.20                    0.34 0.34 0.66 0.14 2.76
## O1   0.26                          0.50  0.32 0.32 0.68 0.21 1.56
## O2-                                0.41  0.24 0.24 0.76 0.06 2.06
## O3   0.33                          0.59  0.47 0.47 0.53 0.23 1.65
## O4-              0.29             -0.35  0.26 0.26 0.74 0.01 2.38
## O5-                                0.49  0.27 0.27 0.73 0.07 1.40
## 
## With Sums of squares  of:
##   g F1* F2* F3* F4* F5*  h2 
## 2.5 2.2 1.2 1.6 1.5 1.4 4.7 
## 
## general/max  0.53   max/min =   3.94
## mean percent general =  0.23    with sd =  0.14 and cv of  0.58 
## Explained Common Variance of the general factor =  0.24 
## 
## The degrees of freedom are 185  and the fit is  0.63 
## The number of observations was  2800  with Chi Square =  1749.88  with prob <  1.4e-252
## The root mean square of the residuals is  0.03 
## The df corrected root mean square of the residuals is  0.04
## RMSEA index =  0.055  and the 90 % confidence intervals are  0.053 0.057
## BIC =  281.47
## 
## Compare this with the adequacy of just a general factor and no group factors
## The degrees of freedom for just the general factor are 275  and the fit is  4.46 
## The number of observations was  2800  with Chi Square =  12444.25  with prob <  0
## The root mean square of the residuals is  0.14 
## The df corrected root mean square of the residuals is  0.14 
## 
## RMSEA index =  0.126  and the 90 % confidence intervals are  0.124 0.128
## BIC =  10261.48 
## 
## Measures of factor score adequacy             
##                                                  g  F1*  F2*  F3*  F4*  F5*
## Correlation of scores with factors            0.71 0.92 0.73 0.81 0.78 0.80
## Multiple R square of scores with factors      0.51 0.84 0.54 0.66 0.62 0.64
## Minimum correlation of factor score estimates 0.01 0.68 0.07 0.32 0.23 0.29
## 
##  Total, General and Subset omega for each subset
##                                                  g  F1*  F2*  F3*  F4*  F5*
## Omega total for total scores and subscales    0.87 0.77 0.69 0.74 0.69 0.56
## Omega general for total scores and subscales  0.47 0.12 0.35 0.15 0.23 0.18
## Omega group for total scores and subscales    0.24 0.65 0.34 0.59 0.46 0.37
# Specific calls available
omega_bfi$omega.tot
## [1] 0.8746716
omega_bfi$alpha
## [1] 0.8188383
# Can use dplyr piping to retrieve Standardized Alpha value
bfi %>%
  dplyr::select(A1:O5) %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  omega(plot=FALSE) %>%
  .$omega.tot
## [1] 0.8588208

Split-Half Reliability

# Runs split-half reliability estimate on data
splitHalf(<DATA>,
          raw = TRUE,
          check.keys=TRUE,
          use = "complete")

Split-Half Example

# Generate split-half reliability output
splitHalf(bfi[1:25],
          raw=TRUE,
          check.keys=TRUE,
          use = "complete")
## Split half reliabilities  
## Call: splitHalf(r = bfi[1:25], raw = TRUE, check.keys = TRUE, use = "complete")
## 
## Maximum split half reliability (lambda 4) =  0.9
## Guttman lambda 6                          =  0.87
## Average split half reliability            =  0.82
## Guttman lambda 3 (alpha)                  =  0.82
## Guttman lambda 2                          =  0.83
## Minimum split half reliability  (beta)    =  0.57
## Average interitem r =  0.16  with median =  0.13
##                                              2.5% 50% 97.5%
##  Quantiles of split half reliability      =  0.73 0.83 0.87
